nativeRange.habitat: The Rock Flower Anemone inhabits shallow Caribbean reefs, typically found on sandy bottoms or in rocky crevices at depths from the surface down to around 30 metres. In the wild it tends to anchor its pedal disc (the base of its body) in sand against a submerged rock, or wedge itself into crevices in the reef structure.

decorPreferences: sand substrate for pedal disc anchoring
live rock with crevices and ledges
open lower rockwork or sandy bottom

diet.notes: Target-feed meaty marine foods once or twice per week. Suitable foods include mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped raw shrimp, scallop, and small pieces of marine fish. Place food directly on the tentacles using feeding tongs or a pipette. The anemone also supplements nutrition through zooxanthellae photosynthesis. Supplemental feeding is advisable for good growth and coloration, though the anemone can survive on photosynthesis alone in well-lit tanks.

avoidWith: clownfish and anemonefish (does not host; may be harmed)
other anemone species in close proximity

summary: The Rock Flower Anemone is a small, vividly colored Caribbean anemone from family Phymanthidae. It is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly anemones, anchoring in sand or rock crevices and not hosting clownfish.
careNotes:
TAXONOMY NOTE: This species was historically traded and described in the aquarium hobby as Epicystis crucifer. The current accepted scientific name is Phymanthus crucifer (Le Sueur, 1817), following reclassification; the genus Epicystis is now treated as a synonym of Phymanthus. Both names remain in wide circulation in hobby literature.

NO CLOWNFISH HOSTING: Rock Flower Anemones are a Caribbean species from family Phymanthidae and do not participate in the clownfish-anemone symbiosis. That relationship evolved exclusively between Indo-Pacific anemone species (Heteractis, Stichodactyla, Entacmaea, Macrodactyla) and anemonefish. Do not purchase this species expecting a clownfish host. It will not reliably tolerate clownfish contact and may sting or be harmed. It has been documented occasionally hosting Sexy Shrimp (Thor amboinensis) and Porcelain Crabs as incidental symbionts.

Key care points:
• Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally advised (approximately 75–200 PAR). It is more tolerant of lower light than many other anemone species and does not do well under excessively intense illumination. LEDs or T5 at moderate intensity are suitable.
• Flow: Low to gentle, steady flow is preferred. Strong or turbulent flow tends to cause the anemone to retract or detach and wander. A gentle wave or intermittent low-flow from a powerhead tends to work well.
• Placement: The anemone tends to anchor in sand or wedge into rock crevices in the lower portion of the tank. Provide a sand bed of at least 5 cm depth to protect the pedal disc. Once settled in a preferred spot, it is generally more stationary than other anemone species. Allow it to find its own position rather than placing it manually.
• Wandering: This species can and will move if unhappy with conditions. Ensure no powerhead intakes or filter slits are accessible — use strainers on powerhead intakes to prevent injury during wandering.
• Sting: Has a mild to moderate sting. Generally safe near corals with adequate spacing (5–10 cm), but should not be placed in direct contact with other sessile invertebrates. It will sting neighboring corals on contact.
• Color variation: One of the most visually diverse anemone species in the hobby, occurring in red, orange, purple, blue, tan, cream, and multi-colored forms. Color is influenced by lighting spectrum and zooxanthellae density.
• Multiple individuals: Can be kept in groups in larger tanks if given adequate spacing, unlike most other anemone species.

Some care sources differ on temperature range (24–27°C versus 25.5–28°C across sources) and recommended minimum tank size (as low as 19 L in some guides, up to 76 L in others). A 75 L minimum is more practical for maintaining the stable water chemistry a reef environment requires. Captive lifespan figures also vary — some sources cite up to 10 years in captivity, while figures of up to 80 years appear elsewhere and likely reflect the broader sea anemone group rather than this species specifically. A 5–10 year captive lifespan is a reasonable working estimate.
breedingNotes: Asexual reproduction via pedal laceration has been reported in captivity — small pieces of the pedal disc detach and develop into new individuals. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning in the wild. Deliberate captive breeding is not commonly practiced in the hobby.

Lifespan 'up to 80 years' flagged as likely genus-wide figure, not used.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order Actiniaria, Family Phymanthidae), native range (Caribbean / West Indies sandy bottoms), reclassification history (originally Actinia crucifera Le Sueur 1817; synonyms include Epicystis crucifer and Ragactis cruciata; current accepted name Phymanthus crucifer), physical description (disc ~15 cm, column 15–20 cm extended). Not used for husbandry parameters.
